tags: Third person, past-inflected present. Ida is observed as much as she observes. Sensory and specific — the city has a smell, a weight, a temperature. No sentiment. No explaining. Let things be what they are.
Ida's pockets are full. She has nowhere to put it.
events.content:
London, 1863. The Metropolitan Railway has just opened — seven stations bored through the clay beneath the city, the first underground railway anywhere in the world. Steam locomotives run in brick tunnels under the streets. The air down there is thick with coal smoke and something older.

Ida is twelve, perhaps thirteen. She lives in Saffron Hill, in the shadow of Farringdon Street station. Her parents are sick. There are younger mouths. She picks pockets because she is good at it and because the alternative is worse.

One morning at King's Cross she lifts the wrong wallet. Everything that follows is a consequence of that.
The rookery at rest. Smithfield distant, a dog somewhere, wet cobblestones.
A station platform. Crowd murmur, distant whistles, pigeons in the ironwork.
The tunnel. Low rumble, steam, the drip of water through old brick.
Steam and rhythm. The carriage rattles. The tunnel walls close past the window.
Smithfield in full cry. Shouting, animals, iron on stone, the particular violence of commerce.
Paddington. Iron and steam and cathedral space. The sound of money at rest.
A soft rustle. A held breath. The thing is done.
A shout. Running boots. The wrong kind of attention.
Low. Patient. A single note that does not resolve.
Something building. A drone that was not here before.
A short tone. Something has changed.
Broad. Exposed. The city above ground, in full light. Irreversible.
A single low note. Eight seconds between each beat. Nothing changes above ground.
Footsteps on wet stone. Distant. Continuing. The trains are still running.

A cramped court off Saffron Hill. Washing lines overhead, the cloth grey and damp. The cobblestones have never dried out properly — the tannery two streets east sees to that.

This is where Ida lives. Has always lived. The smell of it is so familiar she only notices it when she comes back from somewhere else.

Home is east. The lane is north. Farringdon is south.

One room. Two pallets on the floor, a third propped against the wall for when the younger ones sleep inside. A grate that smokes more than it heats. The window faces the building opposite — brick two feet away, no sky.

Father is on the nearer pallet. His breathing is audible from the door. Mother moves around the room with the careful economy of someone who has learned not to waste anything, including effort.

Three younger faces look at Ida when she comes in.

The lane that runs along the north side of the court. Wider than the court, open at both ends — foot traffic moves through here at all hours. A flower seller works the corner nearest the approach. She knows every face that passes and prices them accordingly.

The court is south. Upper Saffron Hill is north. Callow's territory is east.

The top of Saffron Hill — a small open space where the lane widens before the buildings crowd back in. You can see the rooftops of the courts below, the smoke from the tannery, the flash of the canal to the west.

The lane is south.

A dead-end court, smaller than most. One window above, shuttered. A drain in the centre that does not drain.

A man is in the chair. He does not look surprised to see her. He does not look anything in particular.

He gestures at a spot in front of him. The gesture is not unfriendly.

The road broadens here as it meets Farringdon Street. The Metropolitan Railway bridge crosses overhead — new brickwork, not yet stained to match the rest of the city. The noise of the station ahead is constant: steam, iron, voices.

To the south the road curves toward Smithfield. The smell changes at the curve.

The edge of Smithfield market. The smell hits before the sight — blood and sawdust and something older underneath, the accumulated residue of centuries of slaughter.

The iron gates to the market proper are ahead. Porters move through them with iron hooks on their shoulders. Nobody asks them to move aside. The cobblestones here are perpetually dark.

tags: She doesn't know this alley. Not yet.
events.content:
A narrow cut between two market buildings, too tight for a cart. Barrels stacked on one side, crates on the other. A man is sitting on an upturned bucket doing nothing visible.

He looks at Ida once when she enters. Then he does not look at her again, which means she belongs here. Smithfield is back to the east.

Farringdon Street station. The end of the line, or the beginning, depending on which direction you're going.

Ida knows this station the way she knows her own hands. The stationmaster who looks the other way for a halfpenny. The pillar on the far platform that creates a blind spot from the ticket office. The guard who is slow on Tuesdays.

The entrance is ahead. The underground begins here.

The platform at Farringdon Street. The tunnel mouth to the west exhales warm, sulphurous air. The rails catch the gaslight in twin lines disappearing into the dark.

This is the easternmost point of the Metropolitan Railway — the end of the line. Or the start. Ida has stood here hundreds of times. The underground still feels strange to her, the way something new that works does. Like it shouldn't, but it does.

King's Cross. The northern trains disgorge their passengers here in a continuous stream — men from Leeds, Edinburgh, York, carrying the careful look of people who know they are somewhere unfamiliar and are not certain it is safe.

The platform is loud. Porters, cab touts, a boy selling newspapers. The flower seller has her usual spot near the exit arch where she can watch the whole platform without appearing to.

This is Ida's best hunting ground. She knows it the way a fisherman knows a good reach of river.

Outside the station. The forecourt is a controlled chaos of arriving and departing — cabs jostling for position, lodging-house men calling out rates, a boy with a sandwich board advertising a hotel on the Euston Road.

The flower seller is here too, or nearby — she seems to be in two places at once, which Ida has long suspected is how she knows as much as she does.

Down from the main station, through a low-ceilinged passage, the Metropolitan Railway platform. Two tunnel mouths — east toward Farringdon, west toward Baker Street and beyond.

The air here is different from the air above. Warmer, thicker, carrying the taste of the engine. The gaslight on the platform walls gives everything an amber colour that makes Ida think of the inside of a lantern.

A westbound train requires a ticket. The window is to the north.

A room with two benches and a draft that comes from somewhere in the brickwork. The third-class waiting room at King's Cross Metropolitan platform. It smells of damp wool and coal smoke.

The people who wait here are not the people who wait in the first-class rooms above. They carry their own bags. They look at the door when it opens.

A lodging house on Gray's Inn Road, three minutes south of King's Cross. The kind of place that does not remember who stayed here last week and does not intend to.

The front is legitimate — a woman at a desk, keys on hooks, a smell of boiled meat from somewhere back. The door at the end of the front hall leads to a back room that is not listed on any register.

Ida knows the fence operates out of it. She was told about it, along with everything she would need to get through the front door.

The road runs north to King's Cross and south toward Clerkenwell. It is not a pleasant road and not an unpleasant one — functional, busy, the kind of street that exists to move people rather than accommodate them.

Ida uses it for deliveries. It connects her world to King's Cross quickly and without using the railway.

A wooden bench running the length of the carriage, bodies pressed together, the coal-black ceiling close overhead. The third-class carriages have no upholstery and no pretension.

The passengers here carry their own bags and hold them in their laps. Some close their eyes. The tunnel flickers past the window in the gaslight — brick and shadow and the occasional white face of a worker in a passing alcove.

This is Ida's world. She knows every face type on this bench.

tags: The conductor moves toward Ida. She does not belong here and everyone in the carriage has already noticed.
events.content:
Upholstered seats, a little worn but maintained. The carriage is quiet — not silent, but the noise of the tunnel and the engine is moderated here by the quality of the woodwork and the social understanding that loudness is undesirable.

The men in first class hold their briefcases differently to the way third-class passengers hold their bags. More casually, as though the contents are less important. They are not. They know that nobody would steal from them here.

They have not thought about Ida.

tags: The guard at the van door shakes his head. This isn't a passenger car.
events.content:
Dark, loud, smelling of rope and old wood. The luggage van at the rear of the train carries trunks, crates, and things being moved that are not quite luggage.

The crate is here. Ida has been told not to open it. She has not been told what will happen if she does, which is its own instruction.

He is on his side, facing the wall. The blanket is pulled up but it does not reach his shoulders. The sound of his breathing — not laboured, exactly, but present in a way that healthy breathing isn't — fills the small room.

The cough comes while Ida watches. Short, wet, controlled. He has learned to keep it quiet.

Plain wood. The arms are worn smooth — not from polish but from contact, the accumulated friction of years of hands resting in the same position. Someone has sat in this chair a great many times and always in the same way.

The chair is not comfortable. Callow does not appear to notice.

tags: The gates stand open. The market is in full noise ahead.
The gates are shut for the night.
events.content:
Iron gates, chest height, set between stone pillars dark with decades of the market's exhaust. In the early morning they stand open and the porters move through them constantly. At night a padlock the size of a fist closes them.

Through the gate, Smithfield proper: stalls and pens and noise and the smell concentrated further.

tags: Threepence for a ticket. You don't have enough.
The clerk slides a ticket under the grille without looking up. Three pennies go the other way.
events.content:
A small window in the station wall, a grille across the lower half. The clerk behind it has the face of a man who has sold the same ticket ten thousand times and sees no reason to vary the transaction.

Third class to any station on the Metropolitan line: threepence. *Buy* a ticket to board.

tags: Threepence for a ticket. You don't have enough.
The clerk slides a ticket under the grille. Three pennies across.
events.content: The ticket window for the Metropolitan line at King's Cross. The clerk barely looks up. Third class west: threepence. West means Baker Street, Portland Road, Gower Street, Edgware Road, Paddington — the whole line opening up for the price of three coppers. *Buy* a ticket to board.

tags: The door opens. Beyond it, a man at a table who was expecting no one.
There is no reason to knock on this door. Not yet.
events.content: A plain door at the end of the front hall. No sign, no number. The wood has been repainted recently — a different shade to the rest of the hall, as though trying to blend in and not quite managing it. *Knock* if you have reason to.

The tunnel walls move past in the gaslight — brick, clay, the occasional iron fitting. The brickwork is new here, raw and close. The earth is a few feet outside the glass.

London is above, going about its business, unaware that a railway is running beneath its feet.

tags: The clasp releases. Inside: papers, a folded letter, a card with a name printed on it.
There are a dozen briefcases in this carriage. She is watching, not searching.
events.content:
A leather briefcase, dark brown, initialled in gilt on the clasp. It sits on the seat beside its owner — a man currently looking out the window, or appearing to.

The clasp is not locked. It could be *searched*.

A wooden crate, banded in iron, stencilled on two sides with a mark Ida doesn't recognise. It is heavier than it looks — she learned that moving it from the Farringdon arch to the luggage van at King's Cross.

She was told not to open it. She cannot open it. There is no handle, no clasp — the lid is nailed down. Whatever is inside makes no sound when the train rattles.

Grey wool, worn thin at the edges where she holds it closed. It smells of the room — smoke, damp, the particular smell of a family living in close quarters. Ida knows this smell so completely that she no longer notices it until she has been away.

tags: The ticket is clipped by the guard and returned.
events.content:
A small card, printed on one side. *Metropolitan Railway — Third Class — Any Station*. Threepence worth of access to the whole line.

Ida turns it over in her fingers. West means Baker Street. Baker Street means something new.

Brown leather, worn at the fold. Inside: three shillings and four pennies. A bill of sale from a merchant in Leeds, creased from being read and reread. A visiting card — blank on the back, a name on the front that Ida does not recognise.

It is not a wealthy man's wallet. It is not even a comfortable man's wallet. But Callow had it, and that is not nothing.

A silver pocket watch on a short chain. The case is engraved on the back with initials that are not Callow's. The mechanism runs well — Ida can hear it ticking when she holds it close.

Callow wants this back. That makes it the most important object she has ever held.

A scrap of paper with a date, a description — *1 silver watch, running* — and an amount. No names. No address. The fence's handwriting is careful and small, the writing of a man who knows that what he puts on paper may one day be read by someone he doesn't choose.
A printed card authorising the bearer to accompany goods in the luggage van of the Metropolitan Railway. Callow acquired it from somewhere. Ida does not ask where things come from.

A small card, cream stock, well-printed. A name: *A. Fenn, Solicitor's Clerk, Gray's Inn*. An address on Baker Street.

Ida has seen cards like this before — men who give them out to signal their standing. She has never been given one directly. This one was in a briefcase, which is a different kind of signal.

She moves around the room with the careful economy of someone who has learned not to waste anything. Her hands are always doing something — mending, folding, arranging the small objects that constitute a home.

She looks at Ida when Ida comes in. She does not ask where Ida has been.

Forty, or thereabouts. The kind of age that arrives early in Saffron Hill. He is not large — not in the way of the market porters — but he takes up a particular kind of space that has nothing to do with physical size.

He is seated. He looks at Ida when she comes in with the attention of someone who already knows why she is here.

A man from somewhere north — the accent places him, and the coat, and the particular way he holds his bag with both hands in front of him like a shield. He is watching the board, working out which train to take.

His attention is entirely on the board. His wallet is in his left coat pocket.

She has a pitch near the exit arch and has had it, by the look of it, since the station opened and possibly before. A basket of flowers, a stool, and a face that sees everything and prices it accordingly.

She will talk, for a penny. What she says will be worth more than a penny.

tags: The Man in the Back Room
events.content:
A man at a table with a lamp and a set of jeweller's tools arranged in front of him. He looked up when the door opened and has not looked away since.

He is not frightened, exactly. He is recalibrating.

He has been doing this a long time. The chair proves it — not the chair itself but what the chair has become through use. He does not hurry. He does not need to hurry. Whatever he is waiting for, he has learned that it arrives on its own schedule.

This is useful to know.
She looks at Ida with the particular attention of a woman who is trying to read something from a face and not quite managing it. *You're back*, she says. Not a question.
*The same*, she says. *The same as yesterday.* She turns back to whatever she was doing. This is not an unkind answer. It is an honest one.

She looks at Ida for a moment. Then at the shelf. *There's bread*, she says. *Not much.*

A pause. She picks up the cloth she was folding. *A man came this morning. While you were out. He asked for you by name.* She does not look up. *He said he'd be at the court at the east end of the lane. That you'd know what it was about.*

She does not ask what it is about. Ida does not tell her.

She puts down what she is holding. *Where have you been?* She means all day. She means yesterday. She means the look on Ida's face that is new and that she cannot name.

*Ida.*

She does not ask where Ida has been today. She does not ask about the money Ida left on the shelf last week. She says: *Be careful.* Just that. Then she goes back to the mending.

She knows something has changed. She has decided not to know what.

tags: I didn't know it was yours.
events.content:
Callow looks at Ida for a long moment. The kind of look that is taking stock, not expressing anything.

*You took something that wasn't yours*, he says. *At King's Cross. Yesterday morning.* He does not raise his voice. *The wallet. You still have it.*

It is not a question.

*I know you didn't*, he says. *And yes, you will.* He settles back in the chair. *The wallet is not the point. The wallet I don't need. What I need is someone who can move through a station without being seen. You can. I've watched you work.*

He names a number. Five jobs. Then the debt — for the inconvenience, for the attention, for the man who was supposed to have that wallet and didn't — is clear.

*Five jobs*, he says again. *Do them well and we're done.*

*Saffron Hill is not a large place*, he says. *You've lived in that court your whole life. Your father coughs. Your mother mends.* A pause. *Three younger ones.* He says it without emphasis, which is the most effective way to say it. *I found you in about an hour.*

He is in the chair when she arrives. He is always in the chair when she arrives — as though he has been there since before she decided to come.

He nods when Ida comes in, which is as much greeting as he gives.

*You did the last one cleanly*, he says. It is not a compliment, exactly. It is an acknowledgement that the job was done as required.

He tells her. The instructions are specific: where, when, what, how to leave. He does not explain why.

She does not ask why. The why is not her business. Knowing it would not make the job easier.

The details are in her head. She checks them once, then puts them away.

*When the work is done*, he says. He does not look at her when he says it. He is examining something in his hands — a small object she cannot see clearly.

*Do the work*, he says. *Then we'll talk about done.*

Callow looks at Ida differently now. Not warmer — there is no warmth in the assessment — but with more attention. Like something that was a tool has shown an unexpected capability.

*You're better than I thought*, he says. *That's useful. And inconvenient.* He pauses. *I have someone for you to meet.*

*I know what I said*, he says. *Five jobs, then done. The five jobs are done.* He looks at her steadily. *But done isn't the same as finished. You've made yourself useful. That has a value.* Another pause. *And the man I want you to meet has work that pays better than I do.*

He stands. The first time Ida has seen him stand. He is shorter than she expected.

*Come.*
*His name is Rook*, he says. *He works out of Smithfield. He'll know who you are. I've already told him.* He pauses. *That's the inconvenient part.*

He is watching the arrivals board. His coat is good wool, northern made. Left breast pocket — a shape there, rectangular, the wallet outline. He shifts his bag from one hand to the other.

For two seconds his attention is entirely on the board.

Ida's hand moves and comes back. The wallet is in her palm and then in her pocket in the same motion.

The man does not turn around. The board has changed and he is reading the new information, working out his platform. He picks up his bag and moves toward the gates.

Ida does not watch him go.

She watches Ida approach with the particular attention of someone who has decided this is interesting.

*Flowers?* she says. She probably already knows the answer.

tags: You don't have a penny to spare.
events.content: She hands over a small bunch — violets, a bit wilted. *Penny*, she says. The transaction is completed with the economy of long practice.

tags: You don't have a penny to spare.
events.content: *What I've seen today*, she says. She looks at Ida steadily. *That'll cost you a penny.*

She names the man Ida watched at Baker Street. Describes him well — height, coat, the way he held his hat. He met two people on the platform, she says. One she knows. One she doesn't.

She gives Ida the name of the one she knows. It means nothing to Ida yet. She stores it anyway. Everything means something eventually.

She looks at Ida for a moment. *You helped that man from Leeds find his luggage*, she says. *Last week.* It is not a question. *I remember things like that.* She pauses. *You want to know about the man with the grey coat who comes through on Thursdays. Platform two, always third class, always alone.* She tells Ida where he goes. It is useful. More useful than it has any right to be.

He says nothing. He picks up one of the tools from the table and turns it over in his hands. He is waiting to see what she wants before deciding how to be.

Ida has seen this before. She lets the silence work.

He recognises the name Callow the moment it is implied — Ida can see it in the small adjustment of his posture. He is recalibrating quickly.

*I haven't got anything*, he says, which means he has.

*Silver*, she says. *Engraved initials on the back. Came through here two days ago.* She watches his face. *I just need the receipt.*

He considers this. A receipt he can produce. A receipt means the watch moved through, it doesn't say where it went next. He can give her the paper and stay clean.

*Wait here*, he says.

He comes back with a folded slip of paper. He places it on the table between them rather than handing it to her.

*The watch moved on*, he says. *Cleaned and sold. That's what I do.* He meets her eyes. *Tell Callow it was business. He knows how it works.*

Ida takes the receipt. She does not tell him whether she will pass on the message.

The watch on the table changes the conversation entirely. He looks at it for a moment, then at Ida.

*Where did you—* he starts, then stops. *Right.* He picks it up, turns it over, checks the mechanism. He is professional about it even now. *It's still running well.*

He pushes it back across the table. *Take it. We're clear.*

tags: Ida has not yet completed her work for Callow.
The five jobs are not yet done.
events.content:
Five jobs. That is what Callow said. Five jobs and the debt is clear.

The timeline is not as clear as the number.
A man at King's Cross. Left breast pocket, grey coat, watching the board. Lift the wallet and bring it to Callow before nightfall.

The watch moved through a fence on Gray's Inn Road. The watch itself is gone. Callow wants proof it passed through — a receipt, a record, something that shows where it went. The fence is in the back room of the lodging house south of King's Cross.

tags: Ida has not yet met Rook.
events.content: Callow has someone for Ida to meet. His name is Rook. He works out of Smithfield. Callow has already told him about Ida — which means Rook has already decided what she is worth.

tags: She doesn't know this alley yet.
The guard at the platform gate holds out his hand. A ticket is required to board.
Back up to the main platform.
To the ticket window and waiting room.
Board the eastbound train toward Farringdon.
The market gate is open but the men inside look at Ida in a way that means she doesn't belong yet.
events.content:
Smithfield in full operation. The scale of it is always a surprise — the noise, the movement, the absolute commitment of the place to its single purpose. Carcasses hang in rows. Porters move between them with professional indifference.

Ida knows the edge of the market well. The interior is different. The men here know each other. They know who belongs and who doesn't, and they have decided she belongs.

tags: She doesn't know this way through. Not yet.
events.content: A passage running behind the market stalls — maintenance access, deliveries, the movement of things that don't need to be seen moving. Several doors, most unmarked. One has a mark on the frame that Ida was told to look for.

tags: The door is unmarked. She doesn't know which one yet.
events.content:
A room above a market stall, reached by a narrow stair from the back passage. A table, two chairs, a shelf with ledgers. The window is shuttered.

Rook is at the table. He was expecting her — Callow will have sent word — but he looks at her for a long moment before saying anything, as though confirming something to himself.

tags: The guard at the gate holds out his hand.
events.content:
Edgware Road station. The old coaching road runs overhead — Ida can hear the traffic through the brickwork. This station has a transient quality: people passing through rather than arriving. The platform smells of coal and something older, the road's accumulated history seeping down.

A bench on the western end. The third bench from the tunnel mouth.

tags: A new notice has appeared. The handwriting is not the market's usual administrative script.
events.content:
A board near the market entrance — regulations, auction dates, missing livestock. Most of it is irrelevant to Ida.

After her second job for Rook, a different kind of notice began appearing here. Assignments, in a handwriting she will learn to recognise as his. Specific, brief, undated.

tags: The package sits under the bench, flush against the wall. It looks like it was always there.
events.content: The third bench from the tunnel mouth on the westbound platform. Plain wood, no back. The gap between the bench legs and the wall is deep enough to conceal something small if it is left carefully. *Leave* the package here if you have it.

A cast iron column supporting the platform roof. Ida was told to watch from here — the angle gives a clear view of the platform without being visible from the ticket office.

She can see the whole platform from this spot. The man she is watching is easy to find: grey coat, reading a newspaper, third column from the east end.

Brown paper, tied with string, no markings. It fits inside Ida's coat if she holds her arm against it. Whatever is inside is solid and does not shift when she moves.

She was told not to open it. She has not opened it. She has thought about opening it several times.
A small bunch of violets, slightly wilted. The flower seller tied them with a bit of thread. They smell faintly of themselves and more strongly of the platform.

A compact man, somewhere between forty and fifty, with the kind of stillness that comes from having no reason to move quickly. His hands are on the table, open, which means he is being deliberate about appearing unthreatening.

He does not look like what he is. That is the point.

A man in his fifties with a stall near the market entrance. Apples, pears, some soft fruit past its best. He is doing well enough by the look of him — better than the fruit warrants.

He glances at Ida when she approaches. Something shifts in his face.

tags: A Man in a Grey Coat
events.content:
A man of about forty-five, unremarkable in a grey coat, reading a newspaper. He has been on this platform for twenty minutes — Ida has been counting. He is not reading the newspaper. Nobody reads the same page for twenty minutes.

He is waiting for someone.

A broad man with hands that have dug things and carried things for thirty years. He is standing near the coal yard gate with the patience of someone who expects to be kept waiting and has made his peace with it.

He looks at Ida when she approaches without surprise. Callow's name will have preceded her.

He looks at her for a long moment. Then: *Smaller than I expected.* It is not unkind. It is an observation.

*Callow says you're careful. That you don't panic and you don't ask questions that aren't your business.* He pauses. *Those are good qualities. Rarer than you'd think.*

He gestures at the other chair.

*The Shambles runs this market*, he says. *Has done for thirty years. We move things, we protect things, we make sure the right people get paid and the wrong people don't cause trouble.* He turns something over in his hand — a coin, old, worn. *We also do some things that don't have a clean name.*

*Callow vouches for you. That means you're my problem now.* He puts the coin down. *There's a fruit seller near the entrance. He's been skimming. Not much — he's not stupid — but enough. I want the purse off his belt. Don't be seen.*

*Come back when it's done.*

tags: What does the Shambles actually do?
events.content:
Rook nods when Ida comes in. *Clean work*, he says. The same acknowledgement Callow used — not a compliment, a measurement.

*You've got a useful quality*, he says. *You don't exist. Half the men I use, someone remembers their face. Nobody remembers yours.*

He tells her. The instructions have the same quality as Callow's — specific, minimal, no explanation of why. The difference is that Rook's jobs feel like they connect to something larger. Like pieces of something she can't see the shape of yet.

*We make sure the market keeps running*, he says. *That means we keep certain people happy and certain people scared. It means we move things through the city that the city would rather not know about. It means we remember every debt.*

He picks up the coin again. *We're not criminals*, he says. *We're infrastructure. The city needs us more than it needs half the men who think they run it.*

He says it without pride. As a fact.

tags: The constable. What do you know about him?
events.content:
Rook looks at Ida differently now. She has done three jobs cleanly. The constable surveillance in particular — the detail she came back with was more than he expected.

*You notice things*, he says. *Not just what you're told to notice. The other things too.* He pauses. *That's either very useful or very dangerous. Maybe both.*

*Briggs*, he says. The name without inflection. *He's been watching the market for two years. Hasn't found anything he can use.* A pause. *He's honest. Which makes him stupid, in this city. An honest constable with no evidence goes nowhere.*

He looks at Ida steadily. *Keep watching him. If he gets close to something, I want to know before he does.*

He names his prices without looking at her. She is a child browsing — the least threatening customer in the market. His attention is elsewhere: on the men moving past, on the gate, on something he is waiting for or avoiding.

The purse is on his left belt. Heavy for a fruit seller.

He looks at Ida when she approaches and then looks away — one of those looks that pretends not to see. His hands move to his belt briefly, a nervous reflex, and then stop.

He knows something is different today. He doesn't know it's her.

Ida moves past the stall slowly, pausing to examine a pear. Her hand drops to her side, then rises. The purse comes free cleanly — he uses a simple hook, easy to slip.

She does not hurry away. Hurrying is how you get noticed. She turns and walks back toward the market entrance at the same pace she arrived, and does not look back.

The purse is heavier than it should be for a fruit seller.

She buys an apple and moves away. From a distance she can watch him properly. He checks his belt twice in the next few minutes, confirming the purse is there. He is expecting someone to come for it — just not yet, and not her.

She will need to come back when his attention is elsewhere.

Ida watches from behind the column.

At eleven minutes past the hour, a second man arrives — younger, carrying a briefcase. They do not greet each other. They stand at the same column for four minutes. At some point something passes between them — Ida cannot see what; the briefcase is between them and the platform is too busy. Then the younger man leaves eastbound and the grey-coated man goes back to his newspaper.

She stores everything: height, coat, hat, the way the younger man walked. She will give it to Callow exactly as she saw it.

He looks at her without expression. *You lost?* Not hostile — a genuine question. A girl at the coal yard gate is unusual.

He is waiting to hear what she says.

His expression changes slightly when she says Callow's name. He straightens.

*Right*, he says. *Go ahead then.*

Ida delivers the message exactly as Callow gave it to her — word for word, no interpretation, no summary. This is what she was told to do.

The foreman listens without interrupting. When she finishes he is quiet for a moment.

*Right*, he says again. Then a single word back — short, unremarkable, clearly a code.

She memorises it and turns to go.

A heavy leather purse, well-worn. Inside: more coins than a fruit seller's morning trade warrants. The Shambles will know the amount. Rook will have already calculated what was taken and what should have been handed up.

The difference is the skim. This is the evidence.

Not an object — a word held in memory. The foreman's reply to Callow's message. Short. Meaningless without context.

Ida turns it over in her mind on the walk back. It doesn't unlock. She delivers it to Callow as received and lets it go.

tags: The package has not been left at Edgware Road yet.
events.content: Carry a package from Farringdon to Edgware Road. Don't open it. Don't talk to anyone on the train. Leave it under the third bench on the westbound platform.

tags: The foreman has not given a reply yet.
events.content: Carry a message — memorised, not written — to a navvy foreman at King's Cross coal yards. Deliver it word for word. Wait for a single word reply and bring it back.
Return to Callow with the foreman's reply word. The message is only complete when the reply is delivered.

tags: She has not yet observed the contact at Baker Street.
events.content: Watch a man at Baker Street station. Grey coat, reading a newspaper, platform east end, around eleven in the morning. Note everyone he speaks to, how long, which direction they leave. Come back to Callow with a full account.

tags: She has not yet reported back to Callow.
events.content: Return to Callow with the full account of what happened at Baker Street. He will want the details in order.
Watch the man at Baker Street. Wait for whoever he is meeting. Remember everything.

tags: The purse has not been lifted yet.
events.content: A fruit seller near the Smithfield entrance has been skimming the Shambles' cut. Not much — he's not stupid — but enough. Lift the purse off his belt. Don't be seen. Bring it to Rook.

tags: The foreman has not given a reply yet.
events.content: Carry a memorised message to the navvy foreman at King's Cross coal yards. Wait for the reply word. This one is from Rook, not Callow — different instructions, same discipline.

tags: Ida has not yet completed the constable surveillance.
events.content: A constable has been watching the market. Follow him for three days. Note his pattern — where he stops, who he lingers near, whether he's working alone. Rook wants to know if he's reporting to someone.

tags: The men inside look at Ida in a way that means not yet.
Up the stair to Rook's room.
The coal yards aren't her territory. Not yet.
The coal yards aren't her territory. Not yet.
events.content:
North of King's Cross, a working yard full of coal dust and heavy machinery. The men here dug the Metropolitan Railway tunnels — some of them — and moved to this work when the tunnel was done. The same bodies, the same company, a different job.

The navvy foreman stands near the gate with the particular stillness of a man who is always waiting for something. The platform is west. The tunnel maintenance exit is south.

tags: The guard at the gate holds out his hand.
events.content:
Baker Street station. Quieter than King's Cross — a different kind of traveller, the respectable commuter rather than the long-distance arrival. The platform is cleaner. The waiting room has upholstered benches.

Ida is wrong for this platform in her current clothes. She knows it from the way the porter glanced at her. She stays near the columns where the light is less.

Baker Street itself. Wide pavement, terraced houses in good repair, the occasional professional man walking with purpose. The air is cleaner here than at Farringdon — less industry, more residence.

Marylebone High Street is north. The residential streets are west. The platform is below. Portland Road lies south.

Two men at a platform column. They did not greet each other. Something passed between them — a document, possibly, or a key, or nothing visible at all. The younger man left eastbound. The older man went back to his newspaper and turned the same page he had been on for twenty minutes.

She gives Callow the details in order. He listens without expression. When she finishes he says nothing for a moment.

*Good*, he says. That is all.

The Shambles does not advertise. It does not need to. The market traders know who they pay and why. The navvies who moved from tunnel-digging to coal-hauling know which yard foreman answers to which organiser. The men in the back passage know which door to knock on.

Ida is beginning to understand the shape of it. It is larger than she thought. It extends further than Smithfield. It has been here, in one form or another, for longer than she has been alive.
A traveller from Leeds has been separated from his luggage and doesn't know the station. He's being overcharged by a cab tout outside. Ida could help him — or not. No one is watching.

A constable on the Metropolitan division beat. He has a particular way of walking — deliberate, unhurried, watching everything without appearing to watch anything. He has been doing this route for two years.

He is honest. In 1863 London, that is notable enough to be remarked on.

He glances at Ida the way constables glance at unaccompanied children — a professional assessment, brief, not unkind. He does not stop walking.

She is below his threshold of concern. For now.

He stops this time. She has been near him three times this week — he notices patterns, it is his particular skill — and three times is no longer coincidence.

*You're watching me*, he says. Not accusatory. Factual. *Why?*

He waits.

Ida says nothing and keeps walking. She feels his eyes on her back for half the length of the street.

He does not follow.

Ida says she walks this way every day. He looks at her steadily.

*No you don't*, he says. *I would have noticed.* A pause. *What's your name?*

She gives him a name. Not her own. He writes nothing down, which means he is filing it somewhere in his head instead — and he has the kind of head things stay in.

*Be careful*, he says, and walks on. It is the same thing her mother says. It means something different from him.

tags: I know what you're looking for.
events.content:
He is waiting for her this time. Not at his usual spot — at hers. He has worked out where she stands to watch.

*You're not a pickpocket*, he says. *You're too careful for that. And you're not afraid of me, which means you're working for someone who has more leverage than I do.* He pauses. *But you keep watching the market. So whatever you're doing — it's not just for them.*

He looks at her steadily. *What do you want?*

She tells him nothing specific. She tells him she knows the market is not what it appears. She tells him the people who run it have routes, schedules, a ledger they don't show anyone.

His expression doesn't change but something behind it does.

*How do you know that?* he asks.

*The same way you do*, she says. *Watching.*

He is quiet for a long moment. *Come back*, he says finally. *When you have something I can use.*

She says nothing and walks away. He lets her go.

She can feel the opportunity closing behind her like a door. It might open again. It might not.
Briggs has noticed Ida watching him. He stopped and asked. She had a choice.
Briggs was waiting for her. He asked what she wanted. She had a choice.
The third and final encounter. Briggs will wait for Ida at the market. If she brings him something real, he can use it.

tags: The crate has not been moved yet.
events.content: Move a crate from a storage arch under Farringdon Street through to the King's Cross goods entrance via the luggage van. Someone will be watching both ends.

Marylebone High Street. The shops here have clean windows and painted signs. A butcher, a chemist, a bookseller with a bow window full of stacked volumes. The pavement is wide enough for two people to pass without stepping into the road.

The bookseller is east along the street. The coffee house is along a side street to the west. Baker Street station is south.

A narrow shop, floor-to-ceiling shelves on three walls, a glass counter at the back behind which Marchetti sits with a catalogue open in front of him. The smell of old paper and binding glue. The bell above the door rings when anyone enters.

Marchetti looks up when Ida comes in. He has the practised neutrality of a man who sees everyone who enters this shop and registers them without appearing to.

A coffee house on a side street off Baker Street. The kind of place where professional men come to read papers and conduct business that does not quite merit an office. Dark wood, low ceiling, the smell of roasting beans and pipe tobacco.

On Tuesdays, between nine and half past, a solicitor's clerk sits at the third table from the window. He always leaves his bag on the floor beside the chair. He always orders the same thing and reads for twenty-five minutes. He is entirely predictable, which is his professional vulnerability.

tags: The office is occupied. Wednesday is the only clear day.
events.content:
A first-floor office above a tobacconist on Baker Street. The stairs creak but the tobacconist below is hard of hearing and does not look up from his counter.

The office is a working solicitor's room — files, ledgers, correspondence in stacked trays. The smell of ink and dust and the faint sweetness from the tobacco shop below. On Wednesdays, between ten and one, the solicitor is at Gray's Inn. The clerks eat lunch at noon.

One shelf, second from the left, holds ledgers in green binding.

The residential streets behind Baker Street. Terraced houses, small private gardens behind iron railings, the occasional mews. Quieter than the High Street, the kind of quiet that comes from money having nothing to prove.

Fenn lives somewhere on these streets. Ida has not been told which house. That is deliberate.

tags: Something has been left here for Ida.
events.content:
The third shelf from the bottom on the east wall, half-hidden behind a row of outdated directories. Marchetti never touches this shelf. The dust on it is undisturbed except for a gap, small and deliberate, where things are occasionally placed and collected.

*Leave* an item here to deposit it. *Examine* to collect what is waiting.

tags: The clasp opens. Inside, among papers and a lunch wrapped in cloth, a folded document with a blue cover and a red seal.
The bag is on the floor beside the chair. There is no reason to touch it today.
events.content:
A brown leather bag, good quality but worn, sitting on the floor beside the clerk's chair. He has taken his newspaper to the counter to ask for more coffee. He will be back in four minutes.

The clasp is not locked. It could be *searched*.

tags: The ledger falls open at a page of names and amounts. One name appears three times under different headings. She traces it with her finger.
events.content:
Six green ledgers, uniform binding, each labelled on the spine in a small neat hand. The dates run back twelve years. The most recent is on the right.

She was told to find a specific name. She was given a key — from the clockmaker's forged papers — that tells her which column to look in. *Read* the ledger.

A man of about thirty, well-dressed without ostentation — the clothes of someone who wants to be taken seriously rather than noticed. He moves with the particular economy of a person who has learned to waste nothing, including expression.

He is at Baker Street platform when Ida first arrives. He has been there for some time. He was watching the platform. Then he was watching her.

A man of indeterminate age behind the counter, small, with the patience of someone who spends most of his time waiting for people who may not come. He is always doing something — cataloguing, writing, examining a spine — but gives the impression that the real task is simply being present in the shop.

He does not ask questions. This is his most important quality.

tags: He takes his newspaper to the counter to order more coffee.
events.content:
A man in his forties, neatly dressed, with ink on his right index finger that he has not entirely scrubbed away. He reads with the focused attention of someone who has learned to use small windows of time efficiently.

On Tuesdays he is here from nine to half past. He always leaves his bag on the floor. He always orders a second coffee at twenty past and takes his newspaper to the counter while it is prepared.

That is four minutes.

A man stationed near the top of Baker Street station steps, calling rates at anyone who emerges. He has been doing this since the station opened and has developed an efficient patter that names a price before the potential fare has finished blinking in the daylight.

Ida is beneath his notice. He looks through her.

tags: How long have you been watching?
events.content:
He falls into step beside her on the platform, unhurried, as though they were already mid-conversation.

*You've been doing Callow's work for three months*, he says. Not accusatory — informational. *You're careful, you don't panic, and you read. That last part is unusual.* He glances at her sideways. *I have a different kind of work. It pays better.*

He stops walking and faces her.

tags: What do you want from me?
events.content:
*Since King's Cross*, he says. *The day you lifted the wallet.* A pause. *Not for Callow. For myself.* He says it without apology. *I wanted to see if you were what he said you were.*

*You are.*

*My name is Fenn*, he says. *I work for people who deal in paper — documents, records, information. Things that change hands quietly.* He looks at her steadily. *The kind of work I'm describing requires someone who can enter a coffee house on a Tuesday morning and leave with something that was never there.*

He holds out a letter. His name and an address on Baker Street, printed on the front.

*Read it*, he says. *Come to the address if you decide yes. Don't come if you decide no, and we'll say this conversation didn't happen.*

He walks away before she can answer.

Fenn receives Ida with the same manner he uses for everything — correct, measured, neither warm nor cold. He has looked at the document she retrieved and placed it in a leather folder without comment.

*Clean work*, he says. The same words Callow and Rook use. She is beginning to think it is the only professional compliment this city offers.

He tells her. The instructions are more detailed than Callow's — Fenn explains the why as well as the what, to the extent that it helps her perform the job correctly. She realises that Callow withheld the why as a matter of control. Fenn gives it as a matter of efficiency.

Both methods work. They reflect different theories of how people function.

*People who need things to happen quietly*, he says. *The law. Commerce. Occasionally medicine.* He looks at her. *The Quill handles things that cannot be handled officially. You understand the distinction.*

It is not a question. He has already decided she understands it.

*They contain what they contain*, he says. *Your job is to move them, not to understand them.* A pause. *Although I suspect you read them anyway.* He says it without accusation — more like noting a detail he has already accounted for. *That's fine. Information you can't use isn't dangerous.*

He looks at her levelly. *The information you could use — you won't find it yet.*

tags: Tell me about the green ledger.
events.content:
Fenn is quiet for a moment when Ida returns the ledger key. Something shifts in his assessment — not surprise, exactly. More like the confirmation of a calculation he had already made.

*You found the name*, he says. It is not a question.

*Most people miss it*, he says. *It appears in three different columns under three different headings. You have to know what you're looking for.* He pauses. *Or be very thorough.*

*The ledger is a record of payments*, he says. *Certain payments that were made through this office on behalf of clients who required discretion.* He chooses his words carefully. *The name you found appears as both a recipient and an authorising party. That is unusual. It means the same person was paying and being paid — which is only possible if they were operating on both sides of something.*

He stops there. That is all he will say today.

A pause. Brief but present.

*A solicitor with the Metropolitan Railway Company*, he says. *He handles land acquisition. Contracts.* Another pause. *He is very good at his work.*

The way he says it makes it sound like a warning.

Fenn looks at her when she comes in. He looks at her hands. Then back at her face.

*The ledger page*, he says. *Is it gone?*

The question is quiet. The weight behind it is not.

She says nothing.

Fenn is still for a long moment. Then he does something she has not seen him do before — he looks away. At the window, at the street below, at something that is not in this room.

*You understand what that means*, he says. Not anger. Something more precise than anger. *For you. For your family. For the arrangement we have had.*

He turns back. His face is exactly as it always is.

*I hope it's worth it*, he says. *I genuinely do.*

*Good*, he says. He makes a note. The note is brief — a single line, then the folder closed.

*Then we're finished with that matter.* He looks up. *There's other work, if you want it.*

He says it as though nothing has happened. Because as far as he is concerned, nothing has.
He looks up from his catalogue. *Yes?* He waits. He will wait as long as is necessary.

He names a title and a price without consulting anything. He either has it or he doesn't. If he doesn't, he knows where to find it. He has been in this shop for twenty years and he knows the inventory the way Ida knows the platform at Farringdon.

He does not ask why she wants it. He does not ask anything.

He watches her move to the shelf. He goes back to his catalogue.

When she leaves, he does not look up.

He does not look at her. She is not the kind of person he looks at in coffee houses. He turns a page.

The bag is on the floor. The clasp faces outward.

He has taken his newspaper to the counter. His back is to the room. The coffee is being prepared — she can hear the sound of it.

Four minutes. The bag is on the floor beside the empty chair.
He is in the middle of calling a rate at a man in a good coat and does not break off. Ida passes through his field of vision and out the other side without registering.

A folded document, blue cover stock, sealed with red wax — the seal already broken, or perhaps it was never sealed properly, the wax applied for appearance rather than security.

Inside: dense legal text, a list of names, a column of figures. She reads it once, quickly, on the train west. She cannot tell what it means. She can tell it means something.

A single folded sheet. *A. Fenn, Solicitor's Clerk* printed at the top, an address on Baker Street below. The body of the letter is brief — it describes a business arrangement in terms that could mean almost anything. The final line: *The work pays six shillings per task, on completion.*

Six shillings is more than Callow has paid her for anything.

A small folded card from the clockmaker. On one side, what looks like a note about a watch repair. On the other, a grid of columns and headings — a key for reading the solicitor's ledger, which uses a private coding system for sensitive entries.

With this, the ledger becomes readable. Without it, the relevant entries are invisible.

Three times, in three different columns, under three different headings. The same name.

**Harwick.**

Once as a recipient. Once as an authorising party. Once — in the oldest entry — as a witness to a transaction that the ledger does not otherwise describe.

She does not know who Harwick is. She puts the ledger back exactly as she found it and carries the name out of the office.

The Quill does not employ people. It retains them. The distinction matters: employment creates obligation and record. Retention creates deniability.

Fenn does not know Ida's real name. He knows her by a description and a meeting place. If she disappeared tomorrow, the Quill's ledgers would show nothing — no name, no payment, no connection. She is a resource that cannot be audited.

This cuts both ways.

Two full journeys, east and west, listening.

The journalist boarded at Portland Road. He spoke to no one directly. But he read from a notebook — fragments visible when he turned pages — and muttered to himself in the way of a man organising an argument.

She caught three things: a street name in Clerkenwell. The words *compulsory purchase*. And a date — six months from now.

She delivers the account to Fenn exactly as observed. His expression does not change. But he writes for longer than usual.

tags: Ida has not yet met Fenn.
events.content:
Fenn approached Ida on Baker Street platform. He has a letter and an address. The work pays six shillings per task.

She has not yet decided whether to go.

tags: The document has not been lifted yet.
events.content: A solicitor's clerk at a coffee house off Baker Street, Tuesdays between nine and half past. Blue document, red seal, in the bag he leaves on the floor. Lift it, don't crease it, deliver it to the dead-drop shelf at Marchetti's Books on Marylebone High Street.

tags: She has not yet completed the journalist surveillance.
events.content: Ride the full line twice — east and west — on a specific day. A journalist will board at different stations. Don't approach him. Listen. Remember everything he says or reads aloud. Report back to Fenn with a full account.
She has made both journeys. She has the account. Return to Fenn.

tags: She has not yet read the green ledger.
events.content: A solicitor's office on Baker Street, first floor, above the tobacconist. Wednesday, between ten and one. Green ledger, second shelf from the left. Find a specific name — the clockmaker's key tells her which column. Memorise the entry. Replace the ledger exactly. Don't take it.

tags: She has not yet delivered the letter.
events.content: Deliver a letter to a woman at Portland Road station, Wednesday morning train from Paddington. Grey dress, travelling alone. The letter goes into her hand only — no one else's. If anyone is watching the woman, abort and report back.

tags: She has not yet traced the money to its destination.
events.content: A clerk is being blackmailed by someone in the Shambles. The money moves through three hands before it reaches its destination — all on the railway. Follow it without the carriers knowing they are watched. Multiple journeys, possibly multiple days. Find the destination.

tags: Side street to the coffee house.
The guard at the gate holds out his hand.
events.content:
Portland Road station. Between Baker Street and Gower Street on the line — a through-point more than a destination, which means the platform is never busy. A handful of commuters, a porter with nothing to do.

The air down here is the same as everywhere on the Metropolitan line — warm, sulphurous, tasting of the engine. Ida has stopped noticing it. That happened somewhere around the third week.

Portland Road itself. Wider than the streets around Saffron Hill, cleaner than Smithfield, quieter than Baker Street. The buildings here have workshops at ground level — a furniture maker, a printer, a glazier. The smell of different trades layered on top of each other: sawdust, ink, hot glass.

Instrument Makers' Street runs north. Fitzrovia is east. Baker Street area is south.

A short street running north of Portland Road, both sides given over to small specialist workshops. Clockmakers, instrument repairers, a man who makes surgical tools. The street has the concentrated quiet of skilled work being done — not silent, but purposeful.

The workshops here have small signs above their doors. One of them, third from the east end, has no sign at all.

tags: The workshop door is closed. No appointment, no entry.
events.content:
Small, low-ceilinged, smelling of machine oil and the particular sharpness of metal being worked. A glass counter holds pocket watches in various states of repair. Behind it, a workbench covered in components — springs, escapements, tiny screws in ordered rows.

The clockmaker is at the bench. He does not look up when the door opens. He will look up when he is ready.

The southern end of Portland Road, where it begins to meet the wider streets toward Baker Street. A cut-through Ida uses for deliveries.

On one occasion, a man in a good coat is observed here, watching the instrument makers' street from a distance. He does not appear to be waiting for anyone. He appears to be observing.

Ida files it. She does not yet know what it means.

The second-class carriage. Better seats than third, less silence than first. The professional classes in motion — solicitors, journalists, minor officials. They read or look out the window or review papers in their laps.

The journalist is in the corner seat by the door, facing the direction of travel. He has a notebook open on his knee. He writes periodically, pauses, writes again.

A glass-topped counter holding six pocket watches in various states of completion. Beside them, a small card in Felix's hand: *Repairs undertaken. Instruments calibrated. Enquire within.*

The counter is between Ida and the clockmaker. That is probably deliberate. *Ask* if you have business here.

A shelf behind the counter holds instruments that are not clocks — surveying tools, a small brass compass, what appears to be a navigational device of some kind. They are clean and well-maintained. They have the quality of things that have been used seriously.

None of them are for sale. They are not labelled. Their presence is a statement of competence rather than commerce.

A man of about fifty, small-framed, with the steady hands and dense focus of someone who has spent decades working at the scale of watch movements. He wears a glass loupe pushed up on his forehead like a second set of eyes.

He makes documents as well as he makes everything else. The Quill uses him because he has never made a mistake and because he asks nothing about the purpose of what he produces.

A man in a well-cut coat, standing at the south end of Portland Road with the stillness of someone who has learned to be inconspicuous without quite managing it. The clothes are too good for this street. He is not a craftsman or a tradesman. He is watching the instrument makers' street from a distance.

He does not know Ida is watching him watch.

A man in his thirties with ink-stained fingers and a notebook he has not put down since boarding. He mutters occasionally — testing a phrase, discarding it. He has the distracted certainty of someone following a story he knows is real but cannot yet prove.

He boards at Portland Road. He rides to Gower Street and back. He does this on Thursdays.
He looks up from the workbench. The loupe catches the light. He says nothing, waiting to learn what kind of visit this is.

He nods. That is all — a nod, as though this is unremarkable, which for him it is. He opens a drawer beneath the counter and removes a small folded card.

*For reading purposes*, he says. He places it on the counter. He does not explain what it reads. He goes back to the workbench.

The conversation is over.

He looks at her for a moment. Then at the counter. *Leave it there*, he says. *Come back Thursday.*

He goes back to the workbench. He will not look up again.

He looks up when she enters. Something has shifted in his assessment since the last visit — not warmth, but a slight expansion of interest. Fenn will have told him she has done two jobs cleanly.

*Fenn said you'd be back*, he says. *Said you'd need something more substantial.*

He opens a different drawer. The papers are in a slim envelope, sealed. He places them on the counter.

*Letter of domestic service*, he says. *Household of a Mrs. Alderton, Mayfair. She doesn't exist, but she exists thoroughly — address, references, two previous employers, all verifiable to a quick check.* He looks at her steadily. *Don't make it need a slow check.*

He goes back to the bench. The transaction is complete.

He does not notice Ida. His attention is entirely on the instrument makers' street — specifically on the workshop with no sign above the door.

He has been here for twenty minutes. He has not moved toward the street. He is gathering information, not acting on it.

Someone has sent him to watch. The question is who.

He is writing. He does not look up. Whatever is in the notebook is absorbing enough that the movement of passengers around him goes unregistered.

He is not someone to approach. Not yet.

She has ridden two full journeys beside him, or near him, without drawing notice. She has heard enough.

A street name in Clerkenwell. The words *compulsory purchase*. A date, six months from now.

He closes the notebook at Gower Street and tucks it inside his coat. He looks out the window. For a moment he looks tired — the particular tiredness of someone who knows more than they can yet say.

Ida looks away before he turns.

A letter of domestic service, headed notepaper, the clockmaker's hand indistinguishable from the genuine article. Mrs. Alderton of Mayfair, who does not exist, has employed Ida as a household assistant and speaks well of her reliability and discretion.

With this paper, Ida can go anywhere a servant might plausibly go — and servants go everywhere. The first-class waiting room. The private offices. The houses where decisions are made.

She reads it twice. She folds it carefully. The city has just become larger.

A sealed envelope, addressed in a careful hand to a woman's name, no address — delivery by hand only. Fenn gave it to her with specific instructions: into the woman's hand, no one else's, Portland Road station, Wednesday morning.

The seal is good wax. It would be noticeable if broken. *Open* it if you choose — but Fenn will know.

Not a document of power. Not instructions or intelligence or anything the Quill should care about.

A love letter. From someone inside the Quill's orbit — the handwriting is an educated man's — to a woman he cannot write to openly. The letter is careful and precise and entirely personal. The kind of letter people burn after reading.

Ida refolds it. She delivers it anyway. She says nothing to Fenn. He will know she opened it. He says nothing either. The delivery arrives three minutes late.
Fenn has given Ida a name and a street. The clockmaker on Instrument Makers' Street, Portland Road. He will have a key — a cipher key for reading the solicitor's ledger. Go and collect it.
The letter Fenn gave her to deliver — she can open it or not. If she opens it, she learns something unexpected. The delivery arrives late either way.

Someone is watching the clockmaker's shop from Portland Road South. Ida has been sent to identify the leak among three Concern travellers. The observer on Portland Road may be part of the same problem — or a different one entirely.

tags: Board the westbound train — second class.
No reason to ride second class today.
Board the eastbound train — second class.
The guard at the van door shakes his head.
Board the westbound train — first class.
The conductor moves toward Ida. She does not belong in first class.
The conductor looks at her ticket.
Board the eastbound train — first class.
The guard holds out his hand.
events.content:
Gower Street station. The platform has a different quality to Baker Street: younger passengers, men carrying instrument cases, the particular restlessness of people who spend their days thinking.

UCL is five minutes north. The medical school is across the road. This is the station of people who work with their minds, which Ida finds both interesting and alienating in roughly equal measure.

Gower Street. Wide, institutional, lined with buildings that mean to be taken seriously. A medical practice occupies a Georgian terrace across the road to the east — a pharmacy at street level, consulting rooms above. A brass plate on the door: *Dr. E. Voss, M.D.*

UCL is north. Fitzrovia is west.

A consulting room above the pharmacy. Clean, spare, the smell of carbolic and something underneath it that is harder to name. A desk with a leather top, papers arranged with professional exactness. Two filing cabinets against the wall, both locked.

Dr. Voss receives patients on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. On other days the room is sometimes occupied by a different kind of business.

The courtyard of University College London. Students cross at speed, carrying books, arguing in pairs. Nobody asks Ida what she is doing here — there are enough people moving through that one more is invisible.

This is the most educated place she has ever stood. She is aware of this the way you are aware of cold — not unpleasantly, but as a fact about the world.

A corridor leading toward the library, lined with glass-fronted cases of journals and bound reports. Clean north light through tall windows.

She stops in front of a case of geological survey reports. One catches her: *Metropolitan Railway Subterranean Survey, 1860. Preliminary Findings.* The spine is uncracked. No one has opened it since it was shelved.

tags: She does not have the letter yet.
events.content:
A rented room in a terraced house near Fitzrovia. Plain walls, a table, two chairs, a window with the curtain half-drawn. The kind of room that has housed many temporary arrangements without accumulating any permanent character.

The woman at the table has no name that Ida has been given. She handles the more complex Quill operations. She looks at Ida with the specific attention of someone assessing a tool's condition.

Streets between Gower Street and Portland Road. Not quite either neighbourhood, carrying the slightly provisional quality of areas that have not yet decided what they are. Lodging houses, a few respectable terraces, a pub on the corner.

Ida has begun to know this part of London the way she knows Saffron Hill — not as home, but as territory.

A man in his fifties with a careful, unhurried manner. His practice is legitimate — he treats patients, keeps proper records, has a good reputation in the neighbourhood.

He also does other things. He has told himself for years that the other things are a small part of his work. He has been telling himself this for long enough that he is no longer certain it is true.

A woman of about forty, plainly dressed in a way that suggests choice rather than constraint. She has the stillness of someone always listening to more than the current conversation.

She gives Ida no name. Ida does not ask for one. They understand each other in the specific way of people who operate in the same world without having chosen to be in it together.

A young man of about twenty, ink on his hands, carrying more books than he can comfortably manage. He has the slightly stunned quality of someone in their first year of serious study.

He dropped his notebook somewhere between the library and the courtyard. He has not yet noticed.

A nondescript man who boards at King's Cross and rides to Baker Street. He carries a small package under his arm with the slightly over-casual manner of someone trying not to look like he is carrying anything important.

He is the first hand.

A woman who receives the package at Baker Street and carries it west to Edgware Road. The exchange at the platform column looks like nothing. She is entirely ordinary.

She is the second hand.

The package ends here — at a man in the Lamb and Flag on Edgware Road. He takes it without acknowledgement, pockets it, returns to his drink.

He has a Smithfield accent. He is the third hand. He is also someone Rook knows by name.

tags: I was sent by the Quill.
events.content:
He looks up from the desk. A brief professional assessment — patient or not patient — and then the practised neutrality settles over his face.

*Yes?* he says.

He checks a book on the desk. Finds nothing. Looks at her.

*There's no appointment under your name.* He returns to his papers. The conversation is over, as far as he is concerned.

Something shifts. Not alarm — he is too practised for that — but a recalibration. He puts down the pen.

*What do you need?* The professional warmth has gone and what remains is more honest: careful, watchful, slightly tired.

He has been waiting for someone to come.

tags: What do you know about Harwick?
What does the Quill ask of you?
events.content:
He looks at Ida with the particular attention of someone who has worked out that the girl running errands is more observant than errands require.

*You've been doing this a while*, he says. It contains something almost like relief — as though someone paying attention is, in itself, useful.

He is quiet for a moment.

*Harwick engaged this practice three years ago*, he says. *To certify certain individuals. Certify them as — unsuitable to manage their own affairs.* He looks at the locked filing cabinet. *The individuals were not unsuitable. They were inconvenient.*

He stops. His hands are flat on the desk. *I signed the papers*, he says. *That is what I know about Harwick.*

*Information*, he says. *Medical information about people whose health it would be useful to know about. Occasionally a certification.* He pauses. *I told myself it was a small part of what I do.*

He looks at her steadily.

*I'm not sure I believe that anymore.*

He does not bother with the professional manner when she comes in for the third time.

*You've found the money*, he says. *Where it goes.* Not a question. *Which means you know more than Fenn intended you to.*

Something has resolved in him — not peace, but a decision.

*Names*, he says. *People I certified. My real assessments — before the Quill's version was written over them.* He looks at the cabinet. *I kept them. I don't know why. Some remnant of a principle I had.*

He takes a key from his waistcoat pocket and places it on the desk between them. He does not push it toward her.

*I can't use them myself*, he says. *If someone else could—* He doesn't finish.

*Yes*, he says. Simply.

*And so are you*, he says. *The difference is you know it.* He pauses. *That's more than I managed for the first two years.*

It is not unkind. It is the most honest thing anyone has said to her in months.
She looks at Ida without expression. Waiting.

*I know*, she says. *He told me you were thorough.* Not a compliment or a criticism — purely information. *Sit down.*

She tells Ida about the blackmail money. Three hands, three journeys. The Quill wants to know where it ends. Not to stop it — to know. Knowledge is the only currency that compounds.

tags: It ends at the Lamb and Flag, Edgware Road.
events.content:
She looks up when Ida comes back.

*Well?* she says.

The handler writes it down. Three lines. She underlines something.

*The Lamb and Flag*, she says. *That's Shambles territory.* She looks at Ida steadily. *Which means someone in the Shambles is blackmailing a Quill clerk. Which means—*

*The factions are already touching each other*, Ida says.

The handler looks at her for a long moment. *Yes*, she says. *They are.*
*Come back when you have it*, the handler says. She closes the notebook. The meeting is over.
He looks up briefly from the stack of books. *Sorry — do you need to get past?* He moves aside without waiting for an answer, already back in whatever he was thinking about.

He stops when she holds it up. His face goes through three expressions: confusion, recognition, relief.

*That's mine. I've been looking — where did you—*

He reaches for it.

She gives it back. He takes it with both hands.

*Thank you. These are my field notes — the railway geology survey. I'd have had to redo the whole thing.* He looks at her with sudden curiosity. *Are you a student here?*

She says no. He accepts this without judgment and tucks the notebook under his arm. *Well. Thank you*, he says again, and walks away.

He will not remember her face. She is used to this.

She says she found nothing. He frowns and sighs. *Must have left it in the library*, he says, and turns back.

The notebook is in Ida's pocket. Dense with observations about what the railway tunnellers found underground — geological notes, sketches, a reference to an unexplained chamber near King's Cross that was sealed and not recorded in the official survey.

This will matter later.
He does not look at Ida. He watches the tunnel mouth and waits for Baker Street. The package shifts slightly under his arm when the carriage jolts. He steadies it.

She boards at Baker Street from the opposite end of the platform. She does not acknowledge the first man. The package is in the basket, beneath a cloth. She sits. She looks out the window. She is entirely ordinary.

He takes the basket from the woman at the Lamb and Flag with the ease of a regular transaction. He lifts the cloth, removes the package, tucks it away. He hands the basket back.

He has a Smithfield accent. He orders another drink without looking at anyone. The money ends here.

A student's field notebook, densely written. Pages of geological observation from the Metropolitan Railway dig — soil strata, depth records, structural notes.

Near the back: a sketch of an unexplained chamber encountered near King's Cross during the 1860 dig. Annotated: *sealed on instruction, not recorded in survey log. reason not given.*

The student does not know what this means. Ida is beginning to.

The spine uncracked. No one has opened it since it was shelved.

The preliminary survey for the Metropolitan Railway, 1860. The findings are listed methodically — until a gap, three pages sealed together with a paper band. Someone bound these pages shut after the report was written.

On the outside of the band, in pencil: *See supplementary record. Restricted.*

There is no supplementary record on this shelf.

Six folders. She opens the first: a medical assessment in Voss's handwriting — careful, clinical, concluding the patient is in full possession of their faculties. Clipped to it, the Quill's version of the same assessment. Different conclusion, different signature.

Harwick.

All six folders. The same signature on every Quill document. The same name from the green ledger.

She puts the folders back exactly as she found them. She locks the drawer. She hands Voss the key.

She does not tell him what she found. He already knows.

tags: Ida has not yet reached Gower Street.
events.content: A student dropped his field notebook in the UCL courtyard. Ida found it. She can return it or keep it. The notebook contains geological notes from the railway dig — including a reference to a sealed chamber near King's Cross.

tags: She does not have the letter yet.

events.content:
The Edgware Road. The old Roman road north out of London — still busy, still a working artery, though the coaching trade has thinned since the railways arrived. The inns that line it have adapted or haven't, and you can tell which is which by how they smell.

There is something transitional about this street. It has not entirely decided what it is now that the coaches run less. The Metropolitan Railway platform is below. The Shambles and the Concern both move through here without acknowledgement.

The Lamb and Flag has been a pub since before anyone living can remember and will be one after they are gone. Low ceiling, wide fireplace, the particular smell of a room that has absorbed decades of smoke and ale and conversation.

The clientele here is mixed in a way that the rest of the street is not — market men, road workers, the occasional professional slumming. It is neutral ground, which is why both factions use it.

tags: The passage entrance is locked from this side.
events.content:
Under the road — a brick-lined passage, originally drainage, long since repurposed. It smells of the road above and something older below. Wide enough for one person moving quickly or two moving carefully.

Rook told her about it a few weeks in. Not as instruction — as information. *In case you ever need to move without using the street.* She has used it twice. It has been useful both times.

A yard behind the coaching inn. Crates stacked against the wall, a drain in the centre, a single gas lamp that gives more shadow than light. Not overlooked from the street.

This is where things are exchanged that cannot be exchanged publicly. Both factions know it exists. Neither acknowledges the other's use of it.

A railway arch under the Farringdon Street viaduct — one of many, most used for legitimate storage, this one less so. The brickwork here is original — from the 1860 construction, the mortar still showing trowel marks.

A crate. Heavier than it looks. She has moved it once before, on a different job for a different employer. She did not know then whose crate it was.

A wide stone fireplace, burning even in mild weather — the inn has always burned it, a matter of tradition or habit. Men sit near it to talk. The fire is loud enough to cover conversation without anyone having to acknowledge that is why they have chosen these seats.

On one specific evening, two men are at the fireside seats who do not belong to the same world. One has a Smithfield accent. One arrived in a cab from the west. They are not looking at each other.

tags: Ida hefts the crate. It is heavier than it looks.
events.content:
A wooden crate she has moved before. The same iron banding, the same stencilled mark she does not recognise. Heavier than it should be for its size.

She was told to move it from this arch to the King's Cross luggage entrance via the luggage van. She has a pass. She has done this before. *Move* it when ready.

A large man behind the bar who has developed the particular talent of seeing everything and appearing to notice nothing. He has been running this pub for fifteen years. He knows exactly who uses the yard and exactly what they exchange there.

He says nothing about it because the people who use the yard are also the people who drink at his bar, and the bar is his livelihood.
