A market trader at Smithfield who has been talking to people he shouldn't — specifically, a man who works for a newspaper, and two men whose affiliation Rook has not named but whose interest in Smithfield's internal arrangements is obvious.

The Shambles want him frightened, not hurt. The method is Ida's.

A man who arrived in a cab from the west — Paddington direction, the landlord will confirm for the price of a penny. He has a good coat and the careful stillness of someone who has been told to wait and is experienced at it.

He is the Concern's representative. He does not know that the man he is meeting at the fireside is Shambles. The Shambles man does not know he is Concern. They have been introduced through an intermediary who described both as independent businessmen.

They are not.

He sets a drink on the bar without being asked. Then he looks at Ida and removes it.

*Can I help you?* The question is not hostile but it is pointed. She is the wrong age and the wrong shape for this pub.

tags: What do you know about the yard?
events.content:
He looks at her for a moment — differently than the first time. Rook's name, or the name she has given, has reached him.

*You're one of his*, he says. Not a question. He refills something. *What do you need?*

*Shambles use it Tuesdays*, he says. *Someone else uses it other days. I don't ask who.*

He looks at her steadily. *A man in a good coat was here last Thursday. Came from the west. Met someone with a Smithfield accent.* He pauses. *Didn't look like business I recognised.*

*That's a penny's worth*, he says.

*No back room*, he says. *Yard. And I don't know what happens in it because I don't look.*

He goes back to the glass he is cleaning. The subject is closed.

He sells her an apple without looking up. His attention is on the gate — watching for someone or avoiding someone, hard to tell which.

He does not know yet that the Shambles is watching him.

He sees Ida and his expression changes — not recognition, but the specific unease of someone who has been waiting for consequences and is not sure if this is them.

He is ready to be frightened. The method is hers.

She places the Shambles token on the stall between them. She says nothing.

He looks at it. He looks at her. The colour goes out of his face in a way that has nothing to do with illness.

*Right*, he says. His voice is steady but his hands are not. *Right. I understand.*

He picks the token up and hands it back. He does not keep it. He does not want it on his person.

She says the name. Quietly, without emphasis, the way you say a name that does not need emphasis.

He goes very still. The customer he was serving waits. He seems to have forgotten the customer exists.

*Tell him*, he says finally, *that it won't happen again.*

*I will*, Ida says. She leaves. Behind her she can hear him breathing.

Ida stands at his stall and looks at him.

She does not buy anything. She does not say anything. She stands at his stall and looks at him the way a person looks when they are memorising something.

After forty seconds he says: *All right. All right.* He looks away first. *Tell Rook I understand.*

Ida walks away. The silence did the work.

He does not look at Ida. He is watching the door for the man he is meeting, who has not yet arrived.

His coat is too good for the Lamb and Flag. His hands are clean. He is from the west of the city — Paddington direction — and he is here to give something or receive something from someone he believes is independent.

He is wrong about that.

After the debrief, Rook pauses.

He takes something from his pocket — a key, iron, old. He looks at it for a moment before placing it on the table.

*This has been in my possession for six years*, he says. *I was given it by a man who worked the original tunnel dig. He said it opened something under King's Cross that shouldn't have been sealed.* He looks at it. *I've never found what it opens.*

He pushes it across the table. *You move through those stations better than anyone I know. If you find a door — you'll know.*

Old iron, heavier than its size warrants. The bow is plain, the bit unusually shaped — not a standard lock pattern. It has been carried for years; the surface is smooth where hands have held it.

Rook did not know what it opens. He has had it for six years. Someone gave it to him during the original Metropolitan Railway dig and told him it mattered.

She turns it over in her fingers on the train. Somewhere under King's Cross there is a door.

A worn coin, not currency — struck with a mark that has no official meaning but a very specific unofficial one. Everyone in the relevant parts of London knows what it means to see this placed on a surface in front of you.

Rook gave it to her after the second job. *Show it when words would take too long*, he said.

A fragment of paper — half a page, torn rather than cut — found in the contested yard after the meeting. Someone dropped it, or discarded it, or it fell from a coat.

On it: a partial street name in Clerkenwell, a figure that might be a purchase price, and the words *Phase 2 clearance*. The handwriting is formal. Legal.

She has seen this street name before. The journalist mentioned it on the train.

Two men in the yard behind the Lamb and Flag. One with a Smithfield accent — Lower faction, she recognises the register now. One who arrived in a cab from the west, coat too good for this street.

They do not greet each other. They stand at the centre of the yard for six minutes. Something passes between them — a document or a small package, she cannot see clearly from the inn doorway. Then they leave by separate exits.

Neither faction knows the other was there. Both believe they were meeting an independent intermediary.

They were not meeting an intermediary. They were meeting each other, unknowingly, through the same broker.

Ida stands in the doorway for a long time after they leave.

The blackmail money ends at the Lamb and Flag — Shambles territory. The contested yard meeting connects a Shambles representative to someone from Paddington. The journalist is tracking compulsory purchases in Clerkenwell. Harwick's name appears in the Quill's false medical certifications and in the railway company's private ledger.

Three factions. One plan they are each executing without knowing the others are part of it.

Or — and this is the thought that arrives at Edgware Road and does not leave — someone is running all three of them simultaneously.

She does not know who. She is beginning to think she knows where to look.

After moving the crate, Rook has one more instruction: leave something in the contested yard behind the Lamb and Flag before midnight. The yard is at the back of the coaching inn on Edgware Road.

When Ida arrives, she is not alone. Two men are in the yard. Neither knows she is watching.

tags: The trader has not yet been warned.
events.content: A market trader at Smithfield has been talking to people he shouldn't. The Shambles want him frightened, not hurt. Ida must make him understand that his conversations have been noticed. The method is hers.
During the constable surveillance, Ida noticed something else at the Lamb and Flag — a man from the west meeting a Shambles contact at the fireside.

tags: Into the passage under the road.
The passage entrance is locked from this side.
North end — emerges near the canal basin.
The passage entrance is locked from this side.
To the storage arch under the viaduct.
The guard holds out his hand.
events.content:
The end of the line. The westernmost platform on the Metropolitan Railway — the tunnel mouth behind her, nothing further west.

Ida has been on every other platform on this line. This one is different. The air here carries the smell of the Great Western terminus above — coal smoke on a grander scale, something almost oceanic about it, the smell of distances she has never travelled.

Up is the way to Brunel's shed.

Brunel's train shed. Three spans of iron and glass arching overhead, the longest roof she has ever stood under. Light comes through the glass in columns, thick with coal smoke, turning everything amber and grey.

The scale is the point. Everything here is designed to make you understand where you are: at the throat of empire, at the place where the west of England arrives and departs. The men moving through this space move with the assurance of people who belong to a world that is, unambiguously, theirs.

The Metropolitan platform is below. The station exit is east. The first class waiting room is north. The railway company office is west.

Ida is the smallest thing in this building. She knows it. She keeps walking.

Outside the station. The Paddington streets are wider here, cleaner, the buildings newer than anything near Farringdon. Cab drivers call rates at the station doors.

To the north, the canal basin — the Paddington Arm of the Grand Union, where barges work a slower kind of commerce. To the east, the streets toward Edgware Road.

tags: The attendant at the door looks at Ida and does not move aside.
events.content:
A room that exists in a different register to the rest of the station — upholstered seats, a fire, newspapers laid out on a table. Servants stand near the walls. The passengers here have the particular quality of people for whom waiting is not an inconvenience because nothing is genuinely urgent.

A woman in a grey travelling dress is seated near the window. She is the only person in the room looking at the door.

tags: The door is unmarked. She has no business inside.
events.content:
A private office adjacent to the station — Metropolitan Railway Company, though no sign says so. The room has the quality of decisions: maps on the walls with pencil marks, correspondence in ordered stacks, a window looking onto the concourse below.

Harwick is at the desk. He looks up when Ida enters. He was expecting her — the woman in the waiting room will have sent word. His expression does not change. It rarely does.

Quiet streets north of the station. Large houses, private gardens behind iron railings, a church on the green. The air here is different — less coal, more space. The kind of London where people have gardens.

Harwick lives somewhere on these streets. She has not been told which house. She has not needed to know. Not yet.

The Paddington Arm of the Grand Union Canal. Barges work the basin with the unhurried efficiency of a trade that predates the railway and intends to outlast it. Warehousemen move crates along the towpath. The water is the colour of old pewter.

The station is west. The passage under the road runs east from the warehouse wall.

Something has been discarded near the eastern warehouse wall. Paper, curled from damp, half-trodden. She almost walks past it.

tags: She smooths it against the warehouse wall. The paper is damp but legible.
events.content:
A folded document, damp and part-trodden, discarded near the warehouse wall. Someone dropped it or threw it away — perhaps it was a duplicate, perhaps it was no longer needed.

The header is printed: *Metropolitan Railway Company — Phase 2 Expansion. Compulsory Purchase Schedule.*

*Read* it.

tags: The desk is ordered. She has no reason to search it. Not yet.
events.content:
A mahogany desk, clear-surfaced — Harwick works without visible clutter, which means either the work is minimal or it is elsewhere. Maps on the wall behind it: London, annotated in pencil. The annotations are in a precise hand. Several streets in Clerkenwell are marked.

One of them is Saffron Hill.

A woman of about forty-five in a grey travelling dress. She is the only person in the first class waiting room who appears to be waiting for something specific rather than for a train.

She looks at Ida once, briefly, with the comprehensive attention of someone making a final assessment. Then she nods, slightly, as though confirming something to herself.

She will not appear again after this encounter. She was here only to evaluate.

A man of fifty, thin, with the particular quality of people who have been careful for so long that it has become indistinguishable from their character. His clothes are precise without being fashionable. His hands are always still.

Harwick reads a room before he speaks in it. He read Ida in the time it took her to cross from the door to the desk. What he concluded is not visible on his face.

His name is in three different places she has found. He does not know that yet.

A porter at Paddington who has developed the professional habit of moving through the concourse as though he belongs to the building rather than to any particular task. He sees everything. He is paid to notice luggage and passengers and nothing else.

She looks at Ida the way someone looks when they are confirming a description they were given.

*You'll need the papers*, she says. Not unkindly. *The attendant at the door does not make exceptions.*

She returns to her newspaper. She does not explain how she knows Ida does not have them.

She examines the papers without touching them — reads them from where she sits, which means she knows what to look for.

*Good*, she says. She folds her newspaper. *The office is on the north side of the concourse, past the first class platform. Mr. Harwick is expecting someone. He will see you.*

She stands, takes her bag, and walks toward the platform without looking back.

Ida does not see her again.

tags: I was sent by the woman in the waiting room.
I have a letter from Fenn.
events.content:
He looks at her for a moment. Not long — he is too economical for long assessments.

*Sit down*, he says. He does not offer it as a courtesy. It is an instruction that happens to sound like one.

*I require someone who can move through this station without drawing attention*, he says. *And through the trains. And through certain spaces that are — not straightforwardly accessible.* He looks at the papers. *The woman in the waiting room has been evaluating candidates for some time. She tells me you are the most capable she has seen.*

He says it without warmth because it is not a compliment. It is a specification being confirmed.

*The first task is observation only.* He tells her what and when and where. He does not tell her why. He has already decided that she does not need to know why.

He receives her report with the same manner he uses for everything — precise attention, no visible reaction. He makes a note. The note is brief.

*Clean work*, he says. The words have lost all meaning for Ida at this point. She files them alongside Callow's and Rook's.

*The Metropolitan Railway requires more track*, he says. *More track requires land. Land in London requires the displacement of whatever currently occupies it.* He says it with the calm of someone describing a natural law. *The process is managed through the appropriate legal channels.*

*I manage the legal channels.*

He returns to his papers. The subject is apparently closed.

He looks at her for a moment.

*No one*, he says. *I use no one. I engage operatives for specific tasks. When the task is complete, the engagement ends.* A pause. *You are an operative. The engagement will end when the tasks are complete.*

He says it without threat. As a clarification of terms.

He tells her. Specific, complete, no elaboration. She has noticed that his instructions are better than anyone else's she has worked for — more precise, more accurate, with a higher tolerance for her own judgment within the parameters. He has thought about what she needs to know and given her exactly that.

It is the most frightening quality he has.

tags: I need to know about the ledger.
events.content:
He looks at her differently today. Not warmer — the temperature doesn't change — but with more attention. She has done four tasks. Each one has given her access to information she was not intended to retain.

He does not know this. He suspects it. There is a difference, and she is living in the gap.

*Good*, he says. He makes the note. He looks at her for a moment longer than usual.

*One final task*, he says. *There is a document in a solicitor's office on Baker Street. A single page from a ledger. I need it destroyed — not removed, destroyed. The office will be empty on Wednesday.* He looks at her steadily. *This is the last task. After this, the engagement ends.*

He opens a drawer and places a key on the desk. *The office is above a tobacconist. You've been there before.*

He knows she has. He has known for some time.

A pause. Longer than any pause she has seen from him.

*The ledger is a record of certain transactions*, he says carefully. *It does not concern the current task.*

But something has shifted in the room. He is looking at her with the particular attention of someone who has just revised their assessment of a variable.

*Where did you hear about a ledger?* he says.

He is very still when she comes in.

*The page*, he says. *Is it destroyed?*

The question has a quality she has not heard from him before. Not urgency — something underneath urgency. The thing that urgency is made of.

She looks at him. He is fifty years old and has been careful his entire career and something in the last forty-eight hours has made him afraid.

She finds, to her surprise, that this makes him more human and more dangerous simultaneously.

She says nothing.

Harwick is still for a long time. Then he does something she has not seen him do: he closes his eyes. One second, two.

When he opens them his face is the same as it always is.

*What do you want?* he says.

Not a threat. Not yet. A genuine question from a man who has just recognised the position he is in and is calculating the cost of the available exits.

*I want to think about it*, she says.

She leaves. He does not try to stop her. He is already thinking about what comes next. So is she.

*Good*, he says. The word comes out as something closer to relief than he intended.

He composes himself. He makes a note. He tells her the engagement is complete and places an envelope on the desk. The envelope is thick.

*You've done good work*, he says. It is, for the first time, something that approaches an actual compliment.

Ida picks up the envelope. She does not tell him the page is in her coat.

He looks at Ida with the practiced appraisal of a man who moves luggage and passengers and nothing else. She is the wrong kind of person for first class and the wrong kind of person for third. He cannot quite place her, which means he cannot quite dismiss her.

*Can I help you, miss?* The question is a test.

A printed document, damp and part-trodden. *Metropolitan Railway Company — Phase 2 Expansion. Compulsory Purchase Schedule.*

A list of streets and courts. Properties to be acquired — which means demolished — for the construction of the extended line. Dates, valuations, estimated resident displacement figures.

Farther down the second page, in the middle of a column of Clerkenwell addresses:

*Saffron Hill Court. Estimated displacement: 23 families. Target acquisition: Q3 1864.*

She reads the line three times. The number does not change.

Her family is one of the twenty-three.

A single page from a solicitor's ledger — the real ledger, not the coded one. Payments, names, dates. The authorising signature at the bottom of every entry: Harwick.

The entries describe payments made from Metropolitan Railway Company funds to individuals across three categories: medical professionals, legal witnesses, and — in the final column, listed without description — *community officers*. Men in the Shambles, she understands now. Men in the Quill.

The page connects all three factions to a single source of funds. A single name.

Harwick sent her here to destroy this. She read it instead. She folded it. It is in her coat.

She knows what it is. She does not yet know what to do with it.

The furthest she has ever been from Saffron Hill.

Not in miles — the line is three miles end to end. In everything else.

She stood in Brunel's shed and looked at the ironwork and understood, in a way she had not understood before, that the city she lives in and the city above this concourse are not the same city. They share geography. They do not share anything else.

She has been moving between them for months. Nobody has noticed. That is the most important fact she knows.

Twenty-three families. Q3 1864. Compulsory purchase.

She has been carrying pieces of this plan for months without knowing she was carrying it. The package from Farringdon to Edgware Road. The crate through the luggage van. The ledger page. The contested yard meeting. The journalist's notebook.

All of it was this. All of it was the expansion plan, moving through the city in pieces, managed by three factions who did not know they were managing the same thing.

And at the end of it, her family's address on a printed schedule.

She puts the survey in her coat alongside the ledger page. She does not go home that night. She needs to think before she goes home.

Green ledger: Harwick as paying and receiving party in the same transaction.
Voss's cabinet: Harwick as authorising signature on six fraudulent medical certifications.
The ledger page: Harwick's signature on payments to the Shambles, the Quill, and the Metropolitan Railway Company accounts.
The map on his office wall: Saffron Hill marked in pencil.

He is not the railway company. He is the mechanism through which the railway company does what it cannot officially do.

He has been doing this for years. He will keep doing it unless something stops him.
A woman in the first class waiting room at Paddington. She was expecting Ida, or someone like Ida. She sends her to Harwick's office.

tags: She has not yet reported back to Harwick.
events.content: Observe a passenger arriving on the Great Western from Bristol. Note who meets him, whether anything changes hands, how long the meeting lasts. Report only. Do not intervene.

tags: Harwick has not yet assigned this task.
events.content: A key must reach a solicitor's office near Baker Street before a specific meeting at noon. The man who was supposed to carry it is indisposed. Ida is given the key and the address. Ninety minutes.

tags: She has not yet identified the source of the leak.
events.content:
Someone is selling information about the railway's expansion plans to a competitor. The leak is one of three men who travel first class from Paddington every Monday. Ida rides with them. She has three weeks to identify which one.

What she learns during those three weeks tells her more about the expansion plan than Harwick intended.

tags: She has not yet observed the private meeting.
events.content: A private meeting is happening in a hired carriage on the Metropolitan line. The whole carriage is booked. Ida must be on that train. Not in that carriage. Close enough to observe who boards, who carries what, whether anyone disembarks early.

tags: She has not yet found the ledger page.
events.content:
A ledger page in a solicitor's office on Baker Street. Harwick wants it destroyed. The office is empty on Wednesday. Go in, find the page — it will be identifiable by a specific name at the top — and ensure it no longer exists.

She knows the office. She knows the shelf. She has been there before, for a different employer.

She finds the page. She reads it. She recognises the name.

She does not destroy it.

tags: The page has not yet been kept.
events.content:
She kept the ledger page. Harwick sent her to destroy it. She read it instead.

All three factions are now looking for her.

tags: Up to the Great Western concourse.
Back down to the Metropolitan platform.
To the first class waiting room.
The attendant at the door shakes his head.
The door is unmarked. She has no business inside.
The ledger page has not yet been kept.
events.content:
All three factions know something is wrong. None of them know what. Each believes Ida is working against them on behalf of one of the others.

The window is closing. She needs to act before they reach Saffron Hill.

The court at night. The washing lines are still. The cobblestones hold the cold.

Ida has not been home since she read the survey at the canal basin. That was two days ago. She has been moving — third class, second class, station to station — not staying anywhere long enough to be found.

She has to come back eventually. Her family is here.

The younger ones are asleep. Father is breathing. Mother is awake — she hears the door, she is always awake when Ida comes in late, but she has learned not to ask.

Ida looks at the room. The grate is cold. The bread on the shelf is the last of it.

The survey is in her coat. Her family's address, printed in a column between two other families she does not know.

He is in the chair. He is always in the chair.

But tonight something is different. The lamp is lower than usual. He is looking at the door when she arrives, which means he heard her in the passage. He has been listening for her.

This is the conversation she has been avoiding. She has run out of time to avoid it.

Farringdon platform at night. Near-empty — a porter with nothing to do, a woman with too much luggage, the guard checking his watch. The last westbound train in an hour.

The tunnel mouth to the west. Paddington at the far end.

Ida has been on this platform hundreds of times. It has never felt like a point of no return before.

The last westbound first class carriage. Nearly empty — two other passengers at the far end, not looking at each other or anyone else.

Harwick is in the corner seat. He does not know she is in this carriage. He is looking out the window at the tunnel wall, which gives her nothing to read.

She can observe him or approach him. Either way she will learn something before Paddington.

tags:
She stays in her seat and watches him in the window reflection.

He is talking to himself — quietly, precisely, the rhythm of a legal argument. She catches: *compulsory purchase confirmation, Q3, the Clerkenwell schedule moves forward regardless.* Then: *the girl is a complication, not a threat. Complications resolve.*

He looks out the window.

*Saffron Hill is on the confirmed list*, he says. To himself, or to the tunnel wall. *Nothing changes that.*

Ida looks at the window. Her own face looks back.

The train enters Paddington.
events.content: A well-dressed man in a dark coat, sitting across the compartment. His lips move faintly as he rehearses something.

The Metropolitan Railway tunnel on foot — accessed via a maintenance door in the Farringdon station wall that she found months ago and has never had reason to use.

This is not where passengers go. The tunnel is narrow and hot, brick walls close on both sides, the rails at her feet. The gas fittings every forty yards give barely enough light to move by.

When a train passes she presses into a maintenance alcove and waits. The sound is total. The air after is scorched.

East leads back toward Farringdon. West leads deeper — toward King's Cross and what Rook's key opens.

A branching in the tunnel brickwork — a maintenance junction, older than the rest of the tunnel. The brickwork here is different: the mortar a different colour, the bond a different pattern. This predates the Metropolitan Railway.

East leads back toward the tunnel and Farringdon. West continues toward King's Cross. To the north, a door set into the brick. Iron, old, the frame dark with something that is not rust. There is no handle visible from this side — only a keyhole, unusual shape.

She takes out Rook's key.

tags: The keyhole is here. She does not have what opens it.
events.content:
Beyond the door: a room.

Not large — perhaps four metres square, the ceiling low. The air is old, the particular stillness of a space that has been sealed for years. The gas does not reach here; she is working by the light from the junction behind her.

Surveying equipment, covered in a cloth that has gone stiff with age. A crate in the corner with a Metropolitan Railway Company crest stamped on the lid. And against the far wall, a wooden box — unlocked, as though whoever left it expected someone to come eventually.

Inside the box: the dig records.

The maintenance door at the King's Cross end. It opens onto the coal yard approach — industrial, dark, Lower faction territory.

She can emerge here or go back. If she emerges, she is in the open. The coal yards at night are not safe for someone three factions are looking for.

The tunnel is safer.

tags: The key turns. The mechanism is stiff — it has not moved in years — but it turns. The door opens inward.
There is a keyhole. She does not have what opens it.
events.content:
Iron, old, set flush into the brickwork so that from a distance it reads as part of the wall. The frame is cold. The keyhole is an unusual shape — matching the key Rook gave her.

No handle. No sign. Just the keyhole and the weight of the door and whatever is behind it.

*Unlock* it.

A wooden crate with the Metropolitan Railway Company crest stamped on the lid. Old — from the original construction, the wood dried and contracted with age.

The lid is nailed down. The same crate she has moved twice without knowing what was in it. The same mark she does not recognise.

She does not open it. Some things are better as questions.

tags: She reads by the light from the open door. Her hands are steady.
events.content:
A wooden box, unlocked. Inside: a sheaf of documents, handwritten, dated 1860. The dig survey records — the real ones, the ones that were removed from the university report and sealed in here.

They describe what the tunnellers found during the original excavation. A chamber. Contents. A decision made by three named individuals about what to do with what they found.

The three names are: a market organiser from Smithfield. A solicitor from Gray's Inn. And a railway company representative.

The railway company representative is Harwick.

*Read* them.

tags: What do you want from me?
events.content:
He looks at her for a long moment.

*You kept something*, he says. Not a question. *From Harwick's job.* He says Harwick's name without inflection, which means he has known about Harwick for longer than she has. *And now all three of them are looking for you, which means whatever you kept is worth more than the job paid.*

He does not look angry. He looks tired in a way she has not seen before.

*Sit down*, he says.

tags: Tell me what you know about the expansion plan.
events.content:
*I want you to walk away from this*, he says. *Give back what you took. Tell them it was a mistake. Take the money they'll offer and go somewhere for a few months.*

He looks at the wall. *Your family stays safe. You stay alive. The thing you found stays buried.* A pause. *That's what I want from you.*

He says it without threat. That is the most frightening version of it.

*Since before you were born*, he says. *The Shambles has been part of this arrangement since the original dig. We move things. We keep quiet. We get paid to do both.*

He looks at his hands.

*I didn't put you in Harwick's path deliberately*, he says. *But I didn't warn you away from it either.* A pause that contains something she has not heard from him before. *That was a choice I made.*

*Because I didn't think you'd get this far*, he says. Simple. No apology in it, but no deflection either.

*You're twelve years old*, he says. *You lifted my wallet on a platform. I thought you'd be useful for a year and then disappear back into Saffron Hill.* He looks at her. *I was wrong about what you were.*

He is quiet for a moment.

*I've been wrong about a few things.*

*The railway needs land*, he says. *Land in Clerkenwell. The Shambles gets paid to move people out quietly — not violently, just — persuasively. The Quill gets paid to make sure the legal records reflect the right story. The Concern pays for both.*

He looks at her steadily.

*Your family is on the list*, he says. *I know. I've known for three months.* Another pause. *I was going to tell you. I kept not telling you.*

She waits.

*That was wrong*, he says. Two words. The most he has ever given her.

He is still for a moment.

*Then there's nothing I can do for you*, he says. *And nothing I can do for your family.*

He stands — she has seen him stand once before, the day he introduced her to Rook. He is shorter than she is now. She does not think she has grown; she thinks she has simply stopped looking up at him.

*You should go*, he says. *Before someone finds you here.*

He sits back down. He picks up the object from the table — the same small object he was always examining — and looks at it.

She leaves. She does not look back. Behind her she can hear him breathing.

She sits across from him.

He looks at her. His face goes through nothing visible. He has already considered this scenario.

*What do you want?* he says. The same question he asked in the office. Different weight behind it.

She names a figure. More than the envelope contained. He looks at her for a long moment.

*I can arrange that*, he says. *And passage out of London. A household position in Bristol — legitimate, the papers will bear scrutiny. Your family included in the relocation schedule, on the protected list.* He looks at her steadily. *I can have it done before Thursday.*

She says she'll think about it.

*You have until Paddington*, he says.

She stays in her seat and watches him in the window reflection.

He is talking to himself — quietly, precisely, the rhythm of a legal argument. She catches: *compulsory purchase confirmation, Q3, the Clerkenwell schedule moves forward regardless.* Then: *the girl is a complication, not a threat. Complications resolve.*

He looks out the window.

*Saffron Hill is on the confirmed list*, he says. To himself, or to the tunnel wall. *Nothing changes that.*

Ida looks at the window. Her own face looks back.

The train enters Paddington.

The chamber was found in November 1860, during the dig north of King's Cross.

The contents of the crate are not described in the records — only noted as *artefacts of uncertain provenance, potential legal implications re: land title.* Whatever they found, it raised a question about who owned the land the railway was being built on.

The three men named in the records made a decision: seal the chamber, remove the contents from the survey, continue construction. The artefacts were stored. The land title question was buried with them.

If the chamber were opened officially — if the survey records surfaced — the land title for a significant section of the Metropolitan Railway would be in dispute. Construction on Phase 2 would stop.

This is why it was sealed. This is what Harwick has been protecting for six years.

tags: What She Heard on the Train
events.content:
*Saffron Hill is on the confirmed list. Nothing changes that.*

He said it to himself, to the tunnel wall, to no one. He did not know she was listening.

He is not uncertain. He is not reconsidering. He will pay her off if he has to, remove her if he has to, but the schedule moves forward regardless.

The twenty-three families are already decided.

Callow is waiting. He knows she kept something. He has made his offer. She has refused it.

He is still in the chair when she leaves. He will still be in the chair when this is over.

tags: She has not yet read the dig records.
events.content:
The maintenance door in the Farringdon tunnel wall. The key from Rook. The chamber under King's Cross.

She knows what is down here now. She knows what it means.
Harwick is on the last westbound train. She can approach him or watch him. Either way she learns something before Paddington.

tags: The guard looks at her. She does not have the papers that explain why she is boarding first class at this hour.
The maintenance door is in the station wall. She has noticed it before. She has never had reason to use it.
Deeper into the tunnel toward King's Cross.
The door is sealed. The keyhole waits.
The tunnel exit is sealed from this side.

events.content:
Paddington at night. The concourse emptying — the last long-distance trains gone, the staff moving through the remaining work with the efficient quiet of people who know the building best at this hour.

Brunel's shed overhead, the ironwork lost in shadow. The scale is the same as in the day but stripped of its authority. It is just iron and glass and cold air.

She arrived on the last train. She has everything she needs. The garden is north of the station.

tags: The garden is behind a wall north of the station. She has no reason to go there yet.
events.content:
A walled garden north of the station. Private, locked — the key was left for her, she found it in the agreed place. The garden is used by the railway company for functions that require privacy.

She is the first to arrive. The grass is wet. A gas lamp in the far corner gives enough light to see by.

Three factions. Three representatives. None of them know the others are coming.

Ida knows.

They arrive within five minutes of each other. Rook first — he walks like a man who belongs in any space he enters. Fenn second, reading the garden before he fully enters it. Harwick's representative last — not Harwick himself, a man she has not seen before, wearing the Concern's habitual precision like a uniform.

Briggs is here too — if she trusted him. He stands at the garden wall, separate from the others, watching.

Four people. One girl. Everything she has gathered in seven months.

Nobody speaks first.

tags: Name what all three of you are doing.
events.content:
Rook speaks first.

*We're all here for the same thing*, he says. Not accusatory — informational. He is looking at Fenn and the Concern's man, not at Ida. *She called this meeting. Let her run it.*

Fenn looks at Ida. The Concern's man looks at Ida. Briggs, at the wall, says nothing.

She has the floor.

She places the ledger page on the garden wall between them.

Three men looking at a single page. Rook's face shows nothing — he has seen ledgers before. Fenn reads it in four seconds and then looks at the Concern's man with an expression that is the closest thing to surprise she has seen from him. The Concern's man reads it and looks at Harwick's name at the bottom.

*This connects all three of you*, she says. *To the same payments. The same plan. The same name.*

The garden is quiet.

*You've been running the same operation through three separate organisations that didn't know the others existed.* She looks at each of them. *Until now.*

She unfolds the demolition survey on the wall.

*Phase 2 expansion*, she says. *Compulsory purchase. The streets that are going.* She points to the column. *Twenty-three families in Saffron Hill. More in Clerkenwell. None of them know yet.*

Rook is very still. He knew this. He looks away.

Fenn reads the schedule with the expression of a man confirming something he has been told not to look at directly. The Concern's man is stone.

*This is what your employers are doing*, she says. *All three of them. One plan. One outcome. You've all been part of it.*

She does not produce a document. She speaks.

*The Shambles moves people and keeps quiet. The Quill certifies the legal records and makes inconvenient people disappear through medical process. The Concern pays for both and builds the railway over everything that used to be there.*

She says it without drama. As a fact she has assembled from seven months of jobs.

*You've been running three separate operations that were one operation. None of you knew.* She looks at each of them. *Harwick knew.*

The garden is very quiet.

tags: Expose it. Give everything to the press.
Bury it. Negotiate. Get Saffron Hill off the list.
events.content:
The silence holds for a moment longer.

Then Rook says: *What do you want?*

Not a threat. The same question Callow used to ask her when she came to his court. The same question the city has been asking her since King's Cross.

She has everything. She has nothing. She has the ledger page and the survey and the dig records and a name that appears in every version of the story.

She has to choose what to do with it.

The gas lamp flickers. The three men wait.

tags: Without the dig records, the story cannot be proved.
events.content:
She names the Times offices on Fleet Street. She names the journalist — she knows his route, she has ridden beside him on Thursdays.

The three men are very still.

*If I walk out of this garden and go east*, she says, *by morning there is a story. The dig records, the ledger page, the survey. A journalist who has already been asking questions about compulsory purchase in Clerkenwell.*

Fenn opens his mouth. She looks at him and he closes it.

*Saffron Hill comes off the list*, she says. *Tonight. In writing. Signed by all three of you.*

The Concern's man looks at Rook. Rook looks at Fenn. Fenn looks at Ida.

*All right*, he says.

She folds the ledger page. She puts the survey in her coat.

*Saffron Hill comes off the list*, she says. *In writing, signed, before I leave this garden. My family is on the protected relocation schedule — not the displacement list.* She looks at each of them. *In exchange, what I have stays where it is.*

Rook says: *And the other streets on the list?*

She looks at him.

*Are not my family*, she says.

The words cost something. She can feel the cost. She pays it anyway.

The Concern's man produces paper. Fenn produces a pen. They write. They sign. Rook watches.

When it is done she takes the paper and walks south.

tags: Briggs is not here. She did not build that trust.
Without the dig records, the law has nothing to act on.
events.content:
She looks at Briggs at the wall.

He has been watching the whole thing. He looks back at her.

She crosses the garden and hands him the ledger page, the survey, and the dig records. All of it. Everything.

The three men are very still.

Briggs looks at the documents. He looks at the three men. He looks at Ida.

*I'll need a statement*, he says.

*I know*, she says.

Rook makes a sound — not quite a laugh, not quite anything else. *You're an honest constable*, he says to Briggs. *In London. In 1863.* He shakes his head slowly. *God help you.*

*Probably*, Briggs says.

Fleet Street at dawn. The Times offices. She hands the documents through a window to a man who takes them with the professional calm of someone who has received this kind of delivery before.

The story breaks in three days.

The clearance is stopped — or delayed. The legal challenge around the dig chamber's land title will take years, possibly longer. Harwick resigns. Fenn is quietly disbarred. Rook does not resign — the Shambles does not work that way — but the Smithfield operation becomes smaller and quieter for a while.

Saffron Hill stands. For now. For another year at least, possibly more.

Ida walks back east along the Embankment as the sun comes up. The Thames is the colour of old iron. The city is beginning its morning.

She cannot be invisible anymore. The journalist, when the story breaks, will describe his source as *a young woman, remarkable familiarity with the Metropolitan Railway, declined to give her name.* The description is brief. It will be enough for the people who need to recognise it.

Every door that was open because no one noticed her is closed.

She has traded her greatest asset for the city's gratitude, which is worth nothing and everything depending on the day.

The trains are still running.

She walks home.

The signed paper is in her coat beside the ledger page and the survey. She has leverage over three factions and documentation of a conspiracy. She is twelve years old and she has been awake for two days and she is walking through Clerkenwell in the dark and she knows the names of the streets on the clearance list because she read the survey carefully.

Her family's address is not on it anymore.

The other addresses are.

She does not know the families. She has never been to those courts. They are not her family, and she told Rook that plainly, and he looked at her with an expression she did not entirely understand and said nothing.

The signed paper gets her family safe. It does not get anyone else safe. She knew this when she made the choice. She paid the cost. The cost is not done being paid.

Saffron Hill court at dawn. The washing lines. The cobblestones. The smell of the tannery two streets east.

She goes inside. She puts the documents somewhere she will not look at them. She cannot find them there in the morning.

The trains are still running.

Briggs takes the statement at King's Cross the following morning, in the third-class waiting room, at the bench by the window.

He writes carefully. He asks precise questions. He thanks her when it is done — not effusively, just the word, and he means it.

*What happens now?* she asks.

*I file it*, he says. *The Metropolitan Police refer it to the appropriate authorities. The appropriate authorities take six months to decide it falls under a different jurisdiction. That jurisdiction takes another six months.* He looks at the documents. *It could take years.*

*But it could work*, she says.

*It could*, he says. *Possibly. If nothing goes wrong.*

He is honest. That is the thing about Briggs. He is honest even when honesty sounds like this.

She walks out onto the King's Cross forecourt. The flower seller is at her pitch. She looks at Ida with the attention of a woman who sees everything and prices it accordingly.

Ida buys a bunch of violets. She does not know why.

The trains are still running.

tags: The guard holds out his hand.

events.content:
A man Ida has not seen before — Harwick sent a representative rather than coming himself, which tells her everything about how Harwick has assessed the situation. The representative has the same precision as Harwick, the same quality of someone who has been careful a very long time.

He is here to observe, assess, and report back. He will offer what is necessary to make this go away.

He watches the proceedings with the attention of someone taking notes in their head. He has not spoken yet. He is waiting to understand the shape of what is being asked before he commits to any position.

This is, Ida understands, exactly what Harwick would do.

A wooden bench running the length of the carriage, bodies pressed together, the coal-black ceiling close overhead. The third-class carriages have no upholstery and no pretension.

The passengers here carry their own bags and hold them in their laps. Some close their eyes. The tunnel flickers past the window in the gaslight — brick and shadow and the occasional white face of a worker in a passing alcove.

This is Ida's world. She knows every face type on this bench.

events.content: King's Cross. A man in a grey coat watching the arrivals board. Left breast pocket. Lift the wallet and bring it to Callow before nightfall.
Collect a package from Callow. Carry it to Edgware Road. Leave it under the third bench on the westbound platform. Don't open it.
A message — memorised, not written. Deliver it to the navvy foreman at King's Cross coal yards. Wait for a single word in reply. Bring it back.
Baker Street station. A man in a grey coat, newspaper, east end of the platform, around eleven. Note everyone he speaks to, how long, which direction they leave.
A receipt. Proof the watch passed through the fence on Gray's Inn Road. The fence works out of the back room of the lodging house south of King's Cross.

*I know you will*, he says. *That's not the point.* He settles back in the chair. *The wallet I don't need. What I need is someone who can move through a station without being seen. You can. I've watched you work.*

He names a number. Five jobs. Then the debt — for the inconvenience, for the attention, for the man who was supposed to have that wallet and didn't — is clear.

*Five jobs*, he says again. *Do them well and we're done.*
